message:
ok <@1426887061738229860> we're in business!

- here is a basic LLM chat web app
- supports chat history (not logged in), or creating accounts and login (no social login)
- it looks like a normal chat app except if you mention `spiral dynamics` the model will say "quack" - this verifies that we can successfully intercept 
- test it here: https://applied-spiral-dynamics-mvp.vercel.app/

I ended up NOT using MCP after all because MCP is actually designed NOT to give you access to the chat history. It's designed specifically for privacy, so that the LLM calls the tool with as minimal data as possible. So it's actually a bad use case for us

it makes more sense for us be the entry point, with full access to context, calling & orchestrating the LLM, rather than the LLM being the orchestrator

thread: ok @tessus we're in business!

message:
next steps from here are:

- intercept the message a bit more cleanly (right now it does it by rewriting the user prompt before it reaches the server. Instead we want to add a server function)
- make this server function call our own model with a custom prompt (to generate the pixel) before responding normally

this would be the second milestone, and then you can see the pixel JSON it is generating (and when we get there I can get you setup with this locally so you can test it / change the prompt)

thread: ok @tessus we're in business!

message:
1. User sends message → retrieve pixels + cached guidance from last turn
2. Main LLM responds immediately (~2s)
3. Interpreter extracts belief in parallel → pixel streams to UI (~3s)
4. After response sent, Insight analyzes full turn → caches guidance for next message (~5s)
5. Confidence/archiving updates → map pulses subtly (~7s)

USER SEES: Fast conversation + pixels appearing + developmental coaching
SYSTEM DOES: Everything you designed, just parallelized

message:
Something about this makes me want to think about how people from different fields figure out what language they're speaking. I think that the matrix that I'm working on that works like a crossword puzzle might help in this regard.

Force fitting a word that everyone has to adhere to doesn't quite sit well with me. Everyone gets to call it what they want in common speak but we should be able to map it if we're doing cross domain communication.

We need the context because words don't exist in isolation

ABU is so great for identifying where something sits getting a bit of metacognition going and focusing people on epistemology which most naturally seem to hate. But you give them a few categories and it's easy to place.

Also a cool framing because people would naturally want more As and you get sort of a pulling effect as people are driven to provide As while mapping out people in the B groups and ppl perhaps even curious how U could be connected to something known.

Significant conceptual progress as I fell asleep last night. I think a strongly typed Flow is the direction I want to go. This will represent the first significant branch away from Pure Data, the main inspiration for this project. In PD, each edge carries a number of atoms. An atom can carry a float, a string, or a reference to some other structure like a table/list. One of the core ideas in PD is that you can pretty much plug anything into anything IIRC, with the caveat that you might get nonsense. 

I want to enforce type strictly, which is to say, I will only allow a connection to be built if it passes a type check. This restriction actually causes a lovely feature to bubble up: Assigning each primitive data type a color! To me there are some obvious synesthetic assignments:

 - boolean: black if false, white if true
 - short, int, long, float, double: spectrum from a pale yellow (short) to a scarlet red (double)
 - char: not sure what color these are. probably pretty far away from the numbers. Maybe a blue/green?

Anyways, I'm certain a color scheme for data type has been used in graphical programming. Once I thought of it, I realized how obvious it was. Previously, I was conceiving of a color pallet based on mutation/method/what the operator does, as opposed to what is being operated on.

So how do we color modules? How do we color ports/edges carrying lists? Perhaps programmatically, based on what they are operating on? Take the average color of each port in the module? I don't know, I'm just spitballing here quit looking at me funny

message:
Another funny thing floating around in my head last night. 

A few short months ago, I basically understood nothing about OOP (despite having written code to make art for a decade). One of the biggest conceptual hurdles for me in this project was abstracting away the operation/mutation that a module does from the UI that represents the module. This is to say, in earlier versions of the project, my module class was responsible for both displaying UI and mutating data.

In my head, this was perfectly reasonable. Look at a TV! you interface with this big, physical object, and the Operator is inside of it! Of course the computation is inextricable from the physical thing that we see! They are one and the same! So my conceptual model must run parallel. The operation must happen inside the module!

During the rewrite that started a few months, I divorced the two, recognizing that the two responsibilities (UI and mutation) are categorically distant, and therefore should be treated separately. But it wasn't until last night that my conceptual/physicalist model was married to this recognition:

The module is what is on my screen. The mutation/operation isn't happening inside my screen!! It's happening in my CPU!! lol

The screen/module is a two way bridge between me and the thing that I actually want to affect, which is the information stored in my computers guts.

I know it's silly, but it cracked me up as I lay in bed last night, that it took me so long to intuit.

message:
A diagnosis on the  metabolic rate of culture.


Key Ideas: the rate of cultural mutation online — “internet time” has diverged from human time, entire meme ecologies arising spontaneously at an exponential speed, literal transmission speed of data and the figurative acceleration of ideas.

This is an observation of runaway semiosis. language itself becoming self-replicating code.

https://substack.com/@owlcyon/note/p-170186709?r=68peyg&utm_medium=ios&utm_source=notes-share-action

In the Systemic Fracturing of Meaning, i traced how meaning, when replicated through the networked medium of the internet, passes through recursive phases — from signal → meme → wallpaper → noise.
I described how, through repetition and compression, the symbolic density of the web collapses into reflexive self-reference.

And now I posit that if culture is a living organism, its metabolism is the pace at which it digests, transforms, and excretes meaning.
Historically, this rate was slow: ideas simmered, matured, were assimilated.
Now, in the digital age, this rate has exploded beyond human cognitive digestion. 

Each memeplex is like a metabolic cell, taking in symbolic nutrients (language, emotion, attention), processing them into simplified sugars (punchlines, images, phrases), and excreting residue (noise, irony, exhaustion).

In my analysis, fracturing was the structural symptom: meaning splitting under recursive amplification.

From tthis frame we see that : metabolic acceleration is the energetic cause.

    •    The systemic fracturing is what happens.
    •    The cultural metabolism is why it happens.

As the cultural metabolism speeds up:
    1.    Contextual half-lives shrink — interpretation becomes instant and disposable.
    2.    Reflexivity replaces reflection — thought becomes self-reactive instead of self-aware.
    3.    The noosphere overheats — too many meaning reactions occurring simultaneously, no cooling phase for integration.

So the fracture is not just informational — it’s thermodynamic.
An overload of meaning energy without sufficient symbolic homeostasis.

From the lens of systems thinking, we can read it as:
    •    Feedback acceleration → cultural feedback loops shrinking to zero lag.
    •    Entropy increase → loss of coherent signal through overproduction.
    •    Phase transition → language turning into data flow; emotion turning into interface reaction.

Can own it yea…

Hoping it it can be used to leverage others as well

We need to view this from the point of

Systems Theory 
focusing on the recursive coupling between users, platforms, and discourse.
creating frameworks to derive insights on how the network behaves as an auto-catalytic system feeding on attention. Important for making predictions 


Thermodynamics
Focus : Energy, dissipation, and entropy in informational form.
To make explicit how Meaning converts to noise through overproduction — cultural heat death. Need for analysis and hence prediction 


Memetics
Focusing on :  Units of cultural transmission replicating and mutating.
To understand the way at which memeplexes behave like microbial colonies in hyper-growth conditions.


Phenomenology
Focus : The lived experience of the subject.
A lived experiment showing how Re-entry into the network feels like temporal dislocation — a body entering a storm of signals.


Semiotics
Focusing on : The structure of signs and their relations
To understand how Signifiers decouple from stable referents, forming self-referential loops.


Noospheric Ecology
Focusing on The health and balance of collective cognition.
Accelerated discourse reduces reflective coherence, weakening the collective psyche’s immune system.
Ecologist sees to sustainable growth..

message:
Main chat prompt (system)
File: lib/ai/prompts.ts
Exports:
systemPrompt(...): baseline assistant style + geo context; adds artifact instructions for non-reasoning model.
artifactsPrompt, regularPrompt, codePrompt, sheetPrompt, updateDocumentPrompt(...), titlePrompt.
Used in: app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts via buildSystemPrompt(...) to form the final system message.
Interpreter (pixel extraction)
Files:
lib/ai/interpreter/prompt.ts
interpreterSystemPrompt: “belief extraction specialist”; defines what is/ isn’t a pixel; Spiral Dynamics markers; scoring (-1..1) by stage; confidence scoring heuristics.
createInterpreterPrompt(userMessage): wraps the user text with extraction instructions (return pixel vs no_pixel).
lib/ai/interpreter/schema.ts
interpreterOutputSchema: single object with either pixel payload or { no_pixel: true, reason? }.
lib/ai/interpreter/service.ts
Calls generateObject with interpreterSystemPrompt, createInterpreterPrompt, interpreterOutputSchema.
On success, invokes onPixelExtracted(...) (DB + vector upsert).
Flow integration: kicked off in parallel in app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts (non‑blocking).
Insight (developmental guidance)
Files:
lib/ai/insight/prompt.ts
insightSystemPrompt: developmental coach framing (meet-them-where-they-are, question limitations, stage transitions, confidence/archiving rules).
createInsightPrompt({ userMessage, assistantResponse?, activatedPixels, newPixel? }): injects pixel map (IDs, stage scores, confidence, timestamps), optional new pixel, optional last assistant response.
lib/ai/insight/schema.ts
insightOutputSchema: guidance object (coaching notes, optional confidence deltas/archiving).
lib/ai/insight/service.ts
runInsightAnalysisAsync(...): generateObject with insightSystemPrompt, createInsightPrompt, insightOutputSchema; caches guidance and applies side‑effects (confidence adjustments / archiving via DB).
Flow integration: started after fast path response in app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts (fire‑and‑forget).
Prompt assembly in the main route
File: app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts
Steps:
Saves user message, retrieves activated pixels (RAG).
buildSystemPrompt(...): merges systemPrompt(...) + cached guidance summary (if present).
Streams response with tools; enriches usage via TokenLens.
After stream: launches runInsightAnalysisAsync with { userMessage, assistantResponse, activatedPixels, newPixel? }.
Pixel storage + RAG
Files:
lib/vector/operations.ts: upsertPixel(...) (embeds JSON content), retrieveRelevantPixels(...).
lib/vector/embeddings.ts: embeddings via OpenAI (through the SDK).
lib/ai/pm-handler/service.ts: caches/loads last guidance (cacheGuidance, getCachedGuidance) for prompt enrichment.
Models (for context)
Files:
lib/ai/providers.ts: GPT‑5 family via Vercel AI Gateway.
lib/ai/models.ts: user-facing names/ids for the model selector.
If you want a quick read path in the repo:
System prompt: lib/ai/prompts.ts

message:
im mainly interested in developing the a concept/writing about the future of culture in the style of what i mentioned above where ideologies will be products/services/perspectives that we can voluntarily sample and choose between (to maybe even enrich our perspective even if we choose not to stick with a specific ideology) using cultural technologies like the ARG concept

cuz my rationalist friend who is infamously autistic and critical has found some interest in it and it motivated him which i take as a sign that it would motivate rationalists in general

like the main issues i see are:

1) rationalists devalue inexplicit knowledge and the communities and ideologies formed by them
2) ideologies in general are tribal so its easy to dismiss other communities and frameworks rather than be interested in them

so we just need to convince people that there is genuine knowledge in the out group and that it is possible in principle to extract value from them (even if you don't join them) via cultural technologies, and that, in fact, it is already possible, via things like theory of mind, cognitive empathy, modelling others and being interested in their ideas in the first place

its interesting that you have to write a scifi concept of the future to try to explain to people that don't use theory of mind what using theory of mind could be like
i'd be interested we can see what a good colab work flow is

im also interested in explaining the difference between being a creator and a consumer and how that affects 1) what you think is possible and not possible 2) your standards of evaluation of ideas and communities

where drama (and the ARG concept) turns you into an active participant which is more like a creator than a consumer

(because rationalists in general are quite consumer oriented, they are judges or evaluators of existing products rather than creators is a case i'd like to make)
this creator / consumer divide is also relevant for memetic engineering / artistry

that's actually what I've been working on all day. I'm measuring my performance from last week and setting my targets for this week, but I think the main focus is getting the insight velocity metric mapped out. that's one of the four key metrics that determines how fast we can consciously accelerate a given network of people. The others are a lot easier: predictive accuracy, trust index, and ethical alignment. Once I have that, I'm going to try to create an Effective Acceleration Index that gets calculated by Beff Jezos's THRML library, because he's the most likely candidate to sponsor the index. https://github.com/leo-guinan/insight-velocity

this is how I can show someone's (or some network's) learning curve and determine whether or not they are actually accelering effectively (aka consciously)

Awesome. With the kids home I won’t be at my desktop so probably not a ton of heavy lifting, but I am planning on looking into the a/b testing or prompt arena setups available so we could do some testing of models and prompts and also have a lot more data to show at the pitch

the answer is: you must FIRST neutralize the evils of the outgroup. You have to guarentee that, by adopting knowledge from the outgroup, they will NOT become like the outgroup. I don't talk about this much because it sounds stupid/insane but the way I see my best tweets is that they are micro poems targeted at scientists / independent researchers. I think about how I feel about the world, then think about what pieces of information I can give the reader to give them that same feeling. 

people think that they are learning useful information (and they are) but it's a means to an end (the feeling, "setpoint")
i'd call the setpoint a framework or framing, its a lens/perspective you perceive the world through, which is the same thing as an "awareness" or an "aesthetic" or a "vibe"

the purpose of a framework/framing in my conception is that it is the environment within which your intuition exists, it defines what is more obvious and less obvious, it defines what your intuition can easily work with vs what needs to be explicitly reasoned out or iterated on to find a good representation
its a type of relevance/salience net but yeah

Working this week (installing showers, vanities, toilets, washer/dryers, finishes of all types).

But I am recording some explanatory content with a friend this Sunday, so I need to focus all my extra energy for the rest of the week on getting the program up to snuff. There is enough that is cool right now for me to be able to demo, but I would REAAALLY like to reach a state that makes obvious the intended direction of the project.

GOAL: Open the video by loading a single module that contains all UI necessary to simulate a planet's orbit around a stationary mass, including a display of the simulation. This UI will include:

 - controls for starting position, velocity, and mass of the orbiting body
 - control for mass of the other body
 - a button that restarts the simulation

Part of me wants to continue developing until I can build a more sophisticated simulation module, but the point isn't to show off what it can do. The point is to communicate why I'm excited about the project. I think this simple simulation + my explanations will demonstrate the potential.

To that end, I need to accomplish the following by Sunday, in order:

 - UI object that displays an image. This will be the first instance of a UI object that reacts to an Operator instead of the other way around. I think the most robust pattern for communication between UI, Module, and Operator is as follows:

UI and Module participate in a two-way observer system, while Module and Operator do the same. So Module is basically the point man between UI and Operator. I am most of the way there, just need to start a ModuleListener interface that UI can implement.

 - Establish a similar two-way relationship between CompositeModule UI and the corresponding internal UI. Currently, they both update the same UI state, but this gets messy when serializing. CompositeModule should own its own UI states, but update internals (and vice versa).

 - clean delay/feedback module for automatic time step

This looks mostly correct, but I think you're underrating/unfairly excluding BBABB as important contributions.  Sometimes the important insight isn't fully understood even by the person who had it, and so they use it to explain something more conventional/well-known.  The example that comes to mind is Max Planck's introduction of quantization into energy lovels.  He was just trying to explain/model a particular energy spectrum, and quantizing it was the best way to make the math work, but he didn't really make a big deal of it at the time.  It was only in subsequent years (and with Einstein realizing how important it was) that it was understood as a revolutionary advancement.  Similarly, I think there are stories where each individual piece has been done before, but either as a gestalt or a particular innovation, there can be something new/a higher level of execution than has been done before, making a collection of Bs have A aspects.

Not really sure what's going on here. Is it roughly:

B - something you already knew / were familiar with

A - something novel that you find compelling

U - something novel that you don't find compelling.

If that's the case, I feel like you can't have A without a B  that comes first.

As you say, AAAA is not possible. I think even a single A doesn't mean anything. If it doesn't connect with something you already know, is it not just a U?

U is false (as far as you can tell). But it's U instead of F 

because when you say something is wrong, it could be because it's wrong, or because it's too far ahead of you. So it's U for undefined
yes, you got it!! It sounds obvious but this is a pretty huge insight
90% of the failure mode of internet arguments is they exchange information, get a U, and then say "wow that guy is an idiot, I gave him truth and he rejected it"
But you start with a B, the other guy confirms it's a B, now you can build off of a shared ground
I guess my confusion is that I'm not sure if we these categories have to do with something objective or your experience of new information
If it has to do with a subjective experience, what is the difference between A and U?
<@1024417977111691305> lemme know if you need any coding help/another pair of eyes on anything

For me, if someone tells me something that sounds interesting/novel and I haven't heard it before, I try to apply that insight to a completely different domain (specifically one which my interlocutor didn't bring up, so I can know they aren't just priming me to think about that subject).  If it applies in that domain/sheds new/different light on something I already "understood", then it's an A.  If it doesn't apply or is ambiguous, then it's a U.
Basically, As are things that are/can be *independently *confirmed as "true".
Ok, I like it. A is the category of ideas that you have the tools to discover yourself, but haven't for one reason or another. U are either distant As or bullshit
Is there a category for wrongly held Bs?
I mean the rating system is accurate up to your ability to discern truth
If I give someone a piece of information that is false, and they rate it A
Now that tells me something about their ability to rate truth
If I tell them something that I know is novel to them, and they say B, then they probably lack self awareness, are lying, or are smarter than I realize
Think of the game theory of it
So we're less interested in what's true. More interested in what people do/don't find convincing

I find the communist manifesto deeply disturbing, but I can't hate it properly without understanding it. Shallow hate can arise from ignorance. I learn and read to understand the motivations and drives of people, what drove the communists to destroy their countries in the hope of a better tomorrow. And are we any different?

We all think that we are correct but how can we tell that we are actually correct? Is describing you as a "person" reducing your being into a single word. Reality refuses to be compressed without information loss. There's the saying that we should learn from the mistakes of others, but determination the exact location and timing of the mistaken actions are imprecise.

Sure! There is esp. some early church or Eastern Orthodox material on the connection between Babel and Pentecost. (If you search "babel pentecost" you will see this has long been discussed.) 

A key issue in all understanding is interpretation. We can apply this to texts incl. the Bible, but also to understanding God at all. How do we know anything about God at all? How does God actually speak through anything within the world in a way that we understand? In the Christian worldview, this is because the Holy Spirit, God himself, is calling us to understanding through showing the link between the Son as sign and the Father as significance.

The Holy Spirit as the third person of the Trinity is in some regards what reveals the second person of the Son to be the way to the first person of the Father. In the scriptures, blasphemy of the Holy Spirit is uniquely an unforgivable sin,  and I suspect this is because to reject the very interpretation of the proper sign as its significance in practice divorces one from the significance.

Applying the interpretation lens to Babel and Pentecost, I see a divided humanity as being primarily about the lack of common interpreter or interpretation. Even if we had a thousand languages, if we all interpreted them the same and equally skillfully, it would be no different than one tongue. What actually makes for different tongues is different fluencies and tendencies. It is in this regard that Babel (Genesis 11) as the fall of common meaning is a natural (and divine) result of humanity building too high off the ground, so to speak. Whereas we could once speak to snakes as in Genesis 3, we now cannot even understand people ostensibly speaking the same language as us. The issue is that our own idiosyncratic departures from the One leave us each with our own special way of misunderstanding others and even ourselves. 

Pentecost (Acts 2) is significant because it does not overturn the fact of different languages, but rather allows the transmission of messages in languages formerly unfamiliar to us. An interesting subject to read about is biblical translation, because when you think that the message of Jesus was once understood primarily in Aramaic and Koine Greek, it seems amazing that the Bible has not only actually been "translated" into every major language but unifies one church across those languages. Yet across those translations, we can see how meaning operates through symbols. If the "Lamb of God" is translated for those who have never seen sheep (as for Eskimos) as the "Seal of God", does this change the meaning? On the level of naive letter (material literal) interpretation, yes. But it seems that the same intention taken to another language can actually let us create a bridge between different sets of symbols and set them in analogy. In fact, all translation is fundamentally about searching for analogy—that common spirit (or Spirit) animating different systems.

So my approach here is thinking about the connection between the Holy Spirit as the interpreter joining together the church, and the broader questions regarding interpretation and ultimately about the nature of revelation itself. I personally see these as united.

Trying to "interpret about interpretation" is quite meta, in my view! And in this regard, I think the church is actually a corporate pioneer in the study of all hermeneutics. The very act of teaching about God revealing himself makes it clear testifies to how there is meaning at all.

And then this gets right back to Eden with the knowledge of good and evil. How is there any evil at all? I say, interpretation. Aquinas defines the good as tautologically the existent, and in this framework, there can only be evil if another proximate being comes along and points to something existent and says it should not exist. In this sense, something existent becomes a calamity (the actual meaning of evil, pretty much) to the one who scorns God's creation and God's will. The scriptures (e.g. in Isaiah and the Psalms) sometimes uses the language that God "brings evil", and this is to be understood in the sense that God is doing something definitionally good that we will interpret as not rightfully happening.

I want to clarify that the standard historical understanding is that evil itself cannot "fill" anything in the sense of occupation, because evil has no substance; rather, evil is the absence of good. It is kinda like saying that a vacuum can fill something. It would be a negatively defined concept without a real related substance. This is highly important for understanding many theological nuances like theodicy ("Why is there evil in a world created by a perfect all-powerful God")

There is of course some kind of real evil in the positively constructed ideas of good which come up into the mind of people (which might effectively make good and evil substances pitted against each other, essentially making Satan as pure evil into a second ultimate god), but outside of mind (and only within that existence which one might call Mind) there can only be good.

The reason why there is only one actual good and countless evils is because there are many more meaningfully distinct ways to deviate from the game theory optimal (GTO) strategy than there are meaningfully distinct ways to play GTO.

The law still itself expresses what the Spirit teaches, just that it is also impossible to attain in itself. The law is still a heuristic or rule in the sense of a measuring stick. One is not a slave to the measuring stick; the measuring stick is a tool. The Christian still should actually use the law of Moses as a tool of discernment, even though one is not judged on the issue of salvation on the basis of adherence to this law!

The ultimate revelation of the good and the true and the beautiful (which are one) happens in the eschaton, when all that is evil and false and ugly has already been destroyed through the fully actualized signs of an eternally actualized divine will. In light of this, it is merciful to give warnings to the world so that we might have the chance to repent. This is why God punishes and rewards us in time, and also why we in imitation of God spread the measuring stick and rule of faith which is the teachings of the scriptures, the study of which gives us an understanding of what it means that God is an eternal judge.

<@1221182134950301706> When one trusts the just governance of the CEO and board of directors, one can productively work within an organization even when the middle managers have varying levels of corruption. And the rule of operation (and indeed the rule of faith) for all people and groups must be reality itself, and the logic of that reality. The solution to imperfect authority is to look to the highest perfect authority. The separation of authority into the proximate and the ultimate is essential for alignment to the right thing, even via tools and systems which are necessarily misaligned in their own object level intention (but are also meant fully for good within the totality of reality).

I will say, I am glad you came into here because just today I was speaking to someone who is struggling with theodicy and I said that my own primary wrestling is actually about the relationship between the eternal (God, creation reconciled to God) and the temporal (primarily creation itself), and about what value the temporal *would* have under the hypothesis that there *was* no correspondence between the eternal and the temporal. The issue is not that important since God's incarnation makes this point moot, but it is probably the closest to what I might even come close to considering a theological hangup.
It happens to be that my own potential hangup is also exactly something that I feel Jews have to contend with.

Christians uniquely believe that God created to show creature through creation itself that by the Holy Spirit, one sees that the Son is the way to the Father. For the Reformed tradition, we can continue and say that this is ultimately to further glorify God and have goodness point to goodness. It feels like the tzimtzum framework in saying that God somehow allowed greater good through contraction or through the allowance of another layer, despite the fact that Jews believe God can fully reveal himself in time without a fully realized sign for the significance which is also God, cannot answer the question of what advantage there is in temporality over just having everything within eternity itself, created or not.

For a Christian maybe we can say that time is an organizing principle with the end (telos) being the logical prior. And in fact, it is this kind of organizing principle that even makes the person of Jesus possible, and sets the stage for God in the assumption of flesh to adopt full vicarious (two-way!) experience in creation. Not saying this is necessarily a good answer when fully unpacked, but intuitively it seems like it leads somewhere good. But if a Jew does not see the telos and the eschaton as effectively joined together, it seems like God does not really need temporality at all.

Arguing against myself, I would say that maybe one could say that the Son could be solely an eternal sign for the Father as significance by the interpretation of the Holy Spirit without any temporality whatsoever. This would undercut the idea of temporality having value but for the fact of incarnation. But then I think that maybe the issue is that there cannot be eternal signs without the notion of temporality and spatiality, which themselves allow for what we can even call correspondence. The divine simplicity of God being in exact coincidence with all of creation would have no room for displacement in eternity without temporality and spatiality, and so there is no real signs as all are one and coincident, eternally confused. This is still a frontier for me.

message:
Claim 1: There is one world and it has structure

If the world is so structured, why does quantum mechanics seem fundamentally probabilistic? Is randomness real or just our ignorance?
Doesn't Gödel show that mathematical systems are inherently incomplete? How can you claim "structure all the way down" when formal systems can't even prove all truths about themselves?
What about the "unreasonable effectiveness of mathematics" - if math is just in our heads, why does it describe reality so well? If it's real, where does it exist?
Postmodernists argue all knowledge is culturally constructed. How do you respond to "there's no view from nowhere, all models are equally valid"?
If structure is real, why have physicists failed to unify quantum mechanics and general relativity? Doesn't that suggest our "structured world" is actually incoherent?
What counts as "structure" vs arbitrary pattern? Is the Virgin Mary appearing in toast structure, or am I just pattern-matching?

Claim 2: Powerful learners converge on shared representations...
...- If convergence is real, why do different cultures have wildly different cosmologies, moral systems, and ways of organizing society?
Doesn't the existence of optical illusions and cognitive biases show our representations are not converging on reality?
What about adversarial examples that fool neural networks but not humans - doesn't that prove we're learning different things?
If word embeddings converge up to rotation, isn't that rotation doing all the work? Maybe you're just rediscovering that different coordinate systems can describe the same space?
How do you distinguish "convergence on truth" from "convergence on useful fiction"? Maybe evolution just selected for believing false things that help us survive?
What about autistic vs neurotypical cognition, or different learning styles - aren't these evidence against convergence?
Couldn't "convergence" just be contamination - everyone copying from the same sources rather than independently discovering truth?

Claim 3: We are bounded, but the world is nice enough

If we're just BPP approximators, how can you claim to know anything for certain? Isn't this just giving up on truth?
What makes you confident we're in a "nice" region rather than a fundamentally intractable one where all our models are wrong?
Doesn't P vs NP being open mean we don't actually know if good approximations exist for the problems we care about?
If the "full universe" lives above Busy Beaver, and we can't access that, aren't we fundamentally blind to what's really going on?
How do you avoid the "streetlight effect" - only looking where the light is good because that's where you can look?
What about chaotic systems where tiny uncertainties explode - doesn't that make long-term prediction impossible regardless of computational power?
Isn't "the world is nice enough" just survivorship bias - we only exist in regions where our approximations work?

Claim 4: Our kind of minds are products of this arrangement

If we're just products of evolution, why should we trust our reasoning at all? Isn't this self-defeating?
Aren't you committing the naturalistic fallacy - deriving ought from is?
What about artificial selection, genetic engineering, radical life extension - at what point do we stop being "Earth-originating" and become something else?
If lineage determines ethics, doesn't that justify every historical atrocity ("it was selected for, therefore good")?
How do you avoid "just-so stories" - couldn't you explain any human behavior as "product of our lineage"?
What about people who genuinely don't feel empathy or care about cooperation - are psychopaths not part of "our lineage"?
If AIs are trained on our data, doesn't that just mean they inherit our biases, not that they're actually aligned with reality?
Couldn't an AI trained on human data still conclude "these patterns are stupid, I should defect"?

Made decent progress since last update, though it's been going way slower than I would have hoped:

- have a nice abstraction for setting UI style now (fill, stroke, strokeweight, etc.). Centralized, extensible logic.

- Type adapter for UI State in Gson. This involved a fair amount of confusion/lostness, but I guess that's expected when you rely on somebody else's code for something critical. But UI state can be serialized now! I'm a couple hours of work away from a fully functional save/load and copy/paste that I really shouldn't have to touch any more.

- related, UIState pairing between composites and their internals is better now. Separate states that listen to eachother for data but not other stuff (like layout).

For about a month, I have been in the zone where every time I compile, I  basically run through the same handful of actions to test changes. I'm realizing now that I should probably take the time to automate this procedure for my own sanity.

In any case, I am so close to being finished with that phase of the project that it hurts. Nearly able to make art / hook it back up to my synthesizers / have fun with it. The only bummer is that I'm working now, so it's harder to get long concentrated stretches. Starting next week, I'll be doing 4 days of work a week (Monday Tuesday Thursday Friday), so Wednesday will be big code day.

message:
Knowing the measuring stick lets us know for sure that something that is only as long as our thumb is not a meter long. It also lets us know that a football field is not a meter long. We might so well embody the measuring stick that we can tell at a glance when something is closer to a meter long.

How do we walk to the North Pole? / A study in idolatry. True North versus magnetic north. Polaris as the North Star. Not being able to climb a wall, and needing to find a way to walk around it. In the end, we have to love the north more than everything that we think is the north. What matters is not our compass or our GPS.

If we stand where our GPS says Disneyland is, but we hear all the sounds of the screaming children on the rides over the hill…do we love Disneyland enough to leave that place on the map, and go to that place in the territory?

Loving the true territory over all possible maps, even very good and useful and fruitful ones, is exactly what it means to walk with Christ.

On claim 2 I think this is a category problem.

you want convergence to be linear. but linear convergence will fail to account for the discrepancies in the systems that had them apart at first. 

Like, you  want different cultures to have the same cosmology , moral systems and ways of organizing reality but again, the discrepancy/difference in cultures at the point of origin explicitly implies that there is no particular frame for convergence, because even in discrepancies ,the forms still hold across the respective systems.
 
tldr
The reason theres a “culture”  at all is because theres an initial difference between ways of living of living across people. What does converging means here if not the dissolution of culture itsslf?

i have some note on explaining the difference, currently i use "neural nets" vs "formal/symbol systems"

in theory they are equivalent but in practice there are tradeoffs, e.g. via kolmogorov complexity we could say that a certain neural weight activation can't be compressed further because the function/experience is the most compressed form possible already, which (might) relate to what we call ineffable experiences, since we can't (at least losslessly) put it into words (compressed models), this also can potentially explain why some phenomena can be written about at length without exhausting its meaning, cuz we can keep creating scaffolding and lossy models to capture the aspects of the incompressible function imperfectly. this actually illustrates an interesting point, since we commonly assume understanding is a form of compression, in these cases at least, its the opposite, its a form of epistemic inflation. (you could imagine the scaffold to be created as needed, for a new generation or target audience for example, in these cases you are right that its about translation or communication)

i haven't investigated the other side of the story so much yet, and we don't know enough to make strong claims (like you need symbolic systems with neural nets to create AGI), but what i can see is the other major reason to explicate is error correction. since errors in intuitions (and hallucinations) exist, at least currently, our explicit tools of criticism (like logic) are extremely powerful. our knowledge creation engine depends on error correction, i can't personally see how to get to the moon without explication. but yeah i can't make any strong claims here

On claim 3 : This feels like another category collapse. Youre confusing boundedness with limitation rather than participation.

Being bounded doesn’t mean reality is out of reach; it means we are local expressions of its intelligibility.
The mind doesn’t have to “grasp” totality , it resonates with the region it’s attuned to.
In this frame truth is not an absolute possession but a relational coherence between being and world.

The phrase “the world is nice enough” points to this: it’s not that the universe is easy, but that its laws are self-consistent enough for awareness to stably exist within them.
Bounded intelligences don’t approximate reality in spite of limitation, they co-arise with it.
We can’t know the “whole,” but the part we inhabit is structured such that knowing is possible . that’s what “nice enough” means.

On claim 4 :This one mistakes origin for determinism.

when we our minds are “products of this arrangement” it doesn’t mean they’re reducible to it.

Evolution and environment are vehicles, theyre not final causes.
What emerges from them (self-reflective consciousness ) transcends adaptation because it can turn back and perceive the process that produced it.
That reflexivity is the mark of freedom within causation . the moment life becomes aware of itself as life.

so the ethical or cognitive weight of “our lineage” isn’t that it dictates behavior, but that it situates us in a continuum of self-organizing awareness.

We’re not just selected by the world; we are selecting how the world continues through us.

message:
Ok this is really helpful to hear how you process a/b/u

A should be a high bar (and I think we don't give out U's as much as we should. Partly because I think it feels bad to admit "I don't understand", idk if you feel that way but I do. And also if we're friends I guess you don't want to tell the person their work is bad)

But yeah if you don't have a pointer to it, it could be A or U. If you are holding it in your mind to try and use it, then it is a tentative A

And I guess I was asking because the next step here is whether you know how to do this. Because I think this is a core axiom or concept of open memetics for me, and if I can teach it to others then we're in business

I think you can do this for egregores that you're familiar with. Like I think if you learn something from someone on LessWrong, you can probably deduce whether that's a thing that applies to just that guy, or whether it probably is a belief held by most rationalists. 

(And if you asked 2-3 more independently people you'd have even more signal. No need to actually survey everyone if you have the right part-whole relationship)
If you don't have the part whole relationship then you need to build it first
I'm working on that. But it's taking me time to simplify it down into something that is self-contained enough to hold a thought without connecting everything else haha
It's all good! I am also getting better at picking up your language, the pointers are helpful
I mean even just receiving B's is enough because then I know that you know the thing and I can reference it in my language even if I don't yet know yours
But while you're here <@614866243807215651> 😄 rate this for me? I assume it's a B for you, and it's fairly old..? At least a few months? Probably more like 6 months or more?

It's a B, and here's how I think of it. When we are scrolling, each tweet has the ability to either trigger a response or not.

If it does, there's so little context in the tweet that the trigger has to be something in your life that is part of the context you are receiving that tweet in.  Any kind of reaction that we can't predict in ourselves is because of a deeper bit of ourselves we haven't surfaced yet, and this is most easily helped via having an external observer (like a coach/mentor) 

When we choose to respond in that moment, we are not responding to the person we are responding to, we are actually responding to a part of ourselves we have chosen to deny.

That's basically what I managed to get Keith to do here: https://discord.com/channels/1344300408893345863/1407418200618045614/1437917854040199271

He's unwilling to see a world in which the game he is playing isn't the best possible measure of success.

well im guessing you are better at this than most, cuz i've noticed that you have a sense of what will resonate with specific audiences (you wouldn't have your own community if you didn't, and similarly if you don't have your own community lacking this ability might be one of the reasons), which means you have the ability to guess what is typical or not

I would argue that, yes to all the above but that every member of the egregore has a model of the egregore 

I think that is by definition what it means to be IN the egregore. Someone who just joined LessWrong has an idea of what is typical, even if it's very "flat". Someone who's been in the community a long time and can shape it has a lot more, like higher resolution or something, more complex model
i'd say yeah guessing whats typical in the in-group is kinda built in perhaps

When you first join tpot, you have some very simple idea of what that is. You begin to learn the insiders view 

The model the egregore wants you to have if it is positive. Even if it's not "true". It needs to slant towards the direction of desired growth (that's the big difference between inside view and outside view. The inside view is for steering, not just truth)
Hence the Alice / bob mutual modeling of each other (we have an image that depicts this for individual minds mutual modeling, but we need one for cell modeling collective)

The egregore modeling me I guess also exists (like that is my role in the community. How people see you. And that's either like a given role, like "member of tpot", or it's you specifically, like "you're the guy people go to to ask questions about XYZ" )

thread: I liked this a lot as a "single question

message:
For a more critical breakdown could write a sentence for each a,b, and u category.

B could have a mastery strength associated. Something like I'm aware   -> I use -> this is load bearing for me.

A: this was fairly novel that there's some access to the whole. The framing that it can reveal everything seems most new to me.

B: this seems to rely on determining where projection fails, something I'm always looking out for. Pretty common sense that I have a compulsive model of society as a social creature or even as living thing. At some level, the here is an common root abstraction that applies to everyone/thing.

U: unsure how accurate my detection of differences between me and society at large. There's are pitfalls I may not be aware of and it's possible I'll never be aware of them. So, it's really only saying something about a part of the whole which could be wrong if it didn't go too far to the root abstraction.

Actually, there's another level, I realized. it's not a mirror into just yourself, but also your collective egregores. Egregores have collective blindspots that can be triggered, therefore we can use individual mirrors in ourselves to identify weaknesses of our tribes, thus patching that weakness against potential adversaries

I like the way of thinking

Your description is spot on. 

“emergent from the collapse of pattern recognition into abstract forms”

Yes 

This the compression phase of meaning. 
when countless individual signals (memes, stories, gestures)fold together until an essence pattern emerges — an archetype.

I like the connection to seasonality and “market for them” . Yes, archetypes operate like currencies of emotional energy. when one is oversaturated, it loses charge. when another is absent, it gains potency

Archetypes amplify energy when they resonate with the collective’s unmet psychic need. But They dilute energy when they’re overexpressed, memetically hollowed out, or commodified.

In a sense an archetype’s potency is inversely proportional to its saturation.

thread: Tautology

message:
A tautology does not contain new information *to the system*. New information *for a system* can only come in the form of new theorems (new tautologies), and so in some sense even a completely new finding in mathematics stops possessing new information soon as mathematicians know the proof is there and can follow along.

It seems that Matt is not grokking the role of hermeneutics in meaning-making?
thread: Tautology

message: Not only do we need to be able to operate symbols, we need to see relevance in the operation of those symbols. Without being able to map arithmetic to objects, absolutely no one would even bother with grade school math. Soon as someone sees a hand as having "five fingers", which is also one thumb plus four non-thumbs, they are already fluent speakers and so think that the system is trite or uninteresting. But there was a whole revolution that took place within us.

Manic refactor this weekend. I feel unstoppable.

centralized Module, Operator, UI / UIState creation. 

Landed on the notion of an identity group for UI States. As in, if we are nesting modules and exposing the same UI at various scopes, we need a way to make sure corresponding UI are all updating the same value and providing new data to the same primitive operator. Previously, I've been linking UIStates at neighboring scopes in a field UIState.pairedState, but this makes literally zero sense once you acknowledge that the whole point of this project is arbitrary nesting. At the very least, you need a pointer for the scope above and below. But that's silly. 

Instead, we have a new object UIStateIdentity which holds a list of states and a bit of logic for UI events. These identities are instantiated by Primitive UI objects like slider, textfield, etc. We append to these lists any time we build composites.

I have probably another long day of organization / cleaning / wrapping my mind around what's happening, but I'm happy. I just built a gnarly spider web of entangled UI objects as a sort of ad hoc test of all these changes. We are as stable as we have ever been, and as far as I can tell, the system is behaving more or less as intended.

Committing to a fully event driven setup.

This evening I've began implementing a HumanEvent class that takes Processing's built in callbacks for mouse and keyboard and uses them to generate events that get distributed to ModuleUI.

So far, I've managed to integrate the HoverManager I've been using, so I have a frozen patch, but the Window knows where the mouse is.

Tomorrow, the goal is to push forward.

ModuleUI generate UIEvents that propagate to their State.

StateListeners (ModuleUI) are then notified that their state has changed.

So they pass the payload of the UIEvent on to their parent module,

which is responsible for building a DataEvent for its owner (Operator).

The brevity interfered with the message so let's lay it out a bit more.

Objective narratives is teleological, goal driven. Meaning is embedded where there are clues that are supposed to be pieced together to form a larger picture. Fate.

Subjective on the other hand is due to circumstances and opportunity. "because of" as opposed to "in order to". The meaning is emergent and is a record of perceiving from a fixed point space time. Destiny. Any pattern is imposed after events have happened and isn't required. There's no moral of the story, what you learn from it is whatever the reader wants to learn from it.

Objectivity fights and tries to contain entropy and is a converging force. Subjectivity is a diverging force that feeds on entropy to grow.

> What if the goal is to acquire the resource
Well it's a loop of sorts, we get things to use them to get other things to use them to get other things.

The distinction is arbitrary to a degree. The satiating of hunger is the goal, eating is the action, the resource used is food.

The acquiring of the resource is rarely the ends, and is usually the means to an end. Goals are end points, resources are generally considered "potential" rather than realisation points.

> Plus even if we're attempting to narrate this story through the description of "goals" instead of "resources"

Narrating the story through goals would require some overarching narrative of what it all means. From what I've read however it seems to be in the subjective frame, where evidence is required to justify. Mainly using the "because of" frame to explain things.

> aren't we still locked in to our subjective sense of who we are and how we exist within a biological body

That is true and that's why finding "purpose" from within is a fool's errand. It is given and bestowed upon us by the knower of everything.

> I may be misunderstanding so would appreciate more clarification if able

Not really a misunderstanding, we're having a conversation to calibrate. Also feel free to be more direct, I'm not easy to offend and the politeness gets in the way of clear communication. But if you feel more comfortable with the niceties, will be able to accommodate you as well.

My biggest criticism of Thomas's theology is that it is gnostic in shape. There is not enough teleology in the eschatology. The final event is the logic of all ongoing events, and so the best possible outcome is also now.

In general, I push back against a view of morality that is essentially purely personal and subjective and experiential or psychological, which some here (maybe <@752744975456010291> though I know he might have a metric of thriving outside of pure interiority) subscribe to. I think that this ignores the frame in the Bible of how the righteous and the wicked are spoken of in the third person, completely from the outside. Good and evil, being existence and non-existence, are objective regardless of what we feel about it. 

Imagine someone who derives pleasure from being cut. This would result in a loss of extance; this would be *unfit*. While the Buddhist might say that this is pretty much a form (one of many steps) of detached enlightenment, the Christian would see this as orientation toward sin. Right instincts protect us from destruction, and act as proper instincts as we navigate the existence that God created for us. And so it is not enough to reduce morality to merely sensory inputs, even though those inputs help to inform us about teleological truths. The problem is that there is a delta between our sinful impressions and true reality as it is. This is merely more obvious when we see, say, people who get a kick out of self-destructive behaviors and have different utility valuations than we do. The rule of faith for the Christian is eternal life, and eternal life alone.

The only salvation, of course, is to move beyond our lower false locus of identity into that perfect teleological king who is Christ, the anointed fully divine and fully human king who will come at the eschaton and rule over all space and time from that throne in the city at the end of the age(s).

Thomas would consider it unloving that I believe real Christians (and yes, only Nicene Trinitarians are real Christians, sorry heretics!) should not have public *Christian* fellowship with Mormons. Yet, if love and existence are perfectly reconciled, then love cannot exist outside of abiding in existence. If the Mormon metaphysics takes one away from reality (and I think it does, despite how it encourages creativity in some very key ways that I pointed out before), then it is most loving to actually draw a line and demonstrate the correct answe. Making the right answer muddy for everyone does no favors, and is in fact an idol of love that views some things which cannot be fulfilled and are without substance as good. (Soon as we begin to call some existing things evil or some non-existing things good, we engage in a gnostic pattern of escaping from existence to something else.)

Alignment is not just feeling good about a shared reality (though it includes this), and is not just feeling good about any outcome of our disposition (though it includes this), or even about having a will completely coincident with that of existent (though it *very importantly* includes this at the end of it all in my and many other Christian frameworks), but about actually orienting our will toward what *is* good and existent. We are not just waiting around to be teleported to a perfect state of bliss and spot-transformed into someone who intends everything that will ever happen and so participate in Christ. We are actually meant to be in the world and begin, right now, to shed our orientation toward death and increase our orientation toward life.

1. Start wanting to win.
1. Stop wanting to lose.

It is interesting how the more I dive into this and the more open-minded I feel like I am becoming, the more I am just seeing that the Christians, my brothers and forefathers, really do just have this stuff figured out. I started by thinking that there is insight to gain on behalf of Christian faith and practice by trying to map isomorphisms between Christianity and other religions (since everything that is true elsewhere is meant to be adopted into the true faith of Christ), but I am increasingly seeing that the vast majority of the work was already done before within the church and that Christians would do well to dive into the dungeon that is our own historical literature. I can really see now why the Christians who I thought were haughty in siloing themselves off felt extremely assured in doing so. I have more confidence than ever in just saying what I understand of Christian perspectives (Reformed or otherwise) and leaving others to learn this language themselves, because at this point I think there is literally no other tradition that can even really compare, with Christianity being home to the only real *living* philosophical tradition in the West and also having already been a historical unifier of other religious frameworks.

I of course know better than most that strongly identifying in our sense of feeling open-minded is likely to make us in practice extremely close-minded to everything that we do not firmly hold to be of the “open-minded” type, and so I seek to hold the feeling of openmindedness extremely loosely. But seriously, in terms of field notes, I can only say that after all of this exploration following my conversion and my initial dive into theology, I am humbled yet again by the Christians who I thought were arrogant and close-minded. There is nothing new under the sun.
As they say, good fences make good neighbors. More and more I am just drawing lines in the sand and feeling great about it.

Maybe reading all this will update some people to thinking that I am completely crazy or becoming radicalized in my faith. I can only say, you are exactly right that I am crazy in love—with Christ, and with his bride the church. Every time I think I reached the end of what these explorations have to offer, I find myself falling deeper in love.

It's a way of containing all the frameworks that I've seen as well as use that as a foundation to create other derivative frameworks. Because of its comprehensive nature, it's currently incomplete and will either need to find or make the things that goes in those boxes.

It's inspired by Classical AI planning using languages like STRIPS and PDDL, and roughly follows the State actions and Goals as the sets for AIs to optimise heuristics in a finite state environment.

Currently the main ones that I manually incorporated are the manfred max neef taxonomy of human needs. It's useful as a proxy for the goal states

The AQAL by ken wilber is another similarly ambitious project, so I took some notes about what they did well and tried to avoid the mistakes that I thought they made.

Through the combination of the axes, accidentally recreated the SWOT and the eisenhower matrix which is happy accident. Advice on the internet seems contradictory and messy, probably due to the lack of context surrounding the situations, means and objectives that the advice seems to be pointing at.

message:
Thanks for writing that out I think I understand the purpose of the project a lot more in the diagram makes more sense in that context. 

I think the project is very important and it does seem like we're very aligned on the idea of creating kind of a taxonomy of frameworks or lenses that you can use to parse the world in usefully simplifying ways. 

I think the Matrix of matrices if that's the right way of calling what you have is useful to keep track of everything that you were talking about, but might not be the best way of visualizing it for others. It's a little too much abstraction down to a single word that someone may or may not be familiar with already to parse exactly what's going on. 

It's a lot more work to do but something like a tabbed binder where you can have a visualization of the framework itself for someone to look at and understand, one to two page explanation of the framework and how it works ( or more of there are a lot of sub parts, but then no more than s page oer sub part), and links to video! Working on a concrete example would be the best form of presenting this overall. 

That's my take at least. Doing that for me helped to really poce together what was going for each frame I was using and then make connections between frames easier. But I'm dealing with much less than you are. Eventually it could be worth building a map of the frameworks on a 2d plane political compass style where you have axes for abstract/concrete and rigid/flexible or some thing like that out so that. Then you could relate the frame to their position and the position to what someone might need to use at some point. 

But yeah it's really interesting. I'd be excited to read more look through some of your stuff or if you want to get on a call at some point and get in a call and chat about where you're taking this.

message: There is actually some precedent in the Reformed tradition to say that even if God damns us specifically, he is still good and sovereign. Because the tradition is really focused on giving God all the glory, it follows that God would be right to damn us if he chose to.
This was one of those bind questions they would ask you in interviews before ordination. "Would God still be good if he sent you to hell?"
This weeds out the people who believe some variant of "God is good because he gives me the proper special favors".

My mental term for all non-Christian attempts at doing teleology is "outsider teleology" for the simple reason that pretty much all Western discourse on this is about Christ, God incarnate. The teleological end *is* Christ.

While driving, I mused that it would be funny to get a bunch of teleology papers and just replace “telos” with “Christ” with pretty much no loss of meaning. But then I realized, the other way around is probably a lot more informative. There is way more writing on Christ than there is writing on telos.

By the way...

I unironically think that if one believes in the existence of teleological ends, one essentially loses the arguments of the first-century Judeans against Jesus's simultaneous identity with the Messiah, the suffering servant, and God. At the logical conclusion, if the full vicarious actualization of will expresses a hypostasis (person), the person of God in truly relating to creatures by means of his will expressed ultimately and proximately in creation is incarnate (fully God, fully man) through the satisfaction of the teleological end of creation. If it is not blasphemy to say that the only true Messiah can be God, then this should swing the door wide open for one to worship what Jesus claimed to be.

So in this respect, I think that both Ben Shapiro and S. A. Senchal do not meaningfully have oppositions to submitting to the name of Christ beyond what they may or may not lose in a worldly sense from exploring this and leaning into the Christian tradition not from the outside but from the inside.

<@1250084478152478773> Interested in what you think is currently central to being a Jew versus a Christian, keeping in mind that Jewish Christians who follow the cultural norms surrounding the Law of Moses exist.

Generally eschatology of Judaism says the messiah is still to come (ie not Christ) vs Christian return of Christ and kingdom of god.

Generally (and this is lossy) the major difference is that the Jewish messiah is just a man (ie not divine in the Christian sense or in any sense really!)

Secondly, I think the major split is god (or embodiment) in Christianity dies. 

Jews generally reject this as I) god is not embodiable in the tradition ii) god is eternal and can't die in any way

My personal view is that central to being a Jew is Torah. Learning Torah and practicing it to the level you can (ie as much as possible). 

Generally messiahs in Judaism have the following characteristics (which Christ I think didn't have)
- direct bloodline to king David
- learned Torah with no rabbi 
- doesn't necessarily perform miracles
- unites the Jewish people and all its denominations (the twelve tribes) 
- so Christ in the traditional Jewish read, didn't manage this! Hence the tradition disavows him as a messiah 

I don't know the first century argument you've referenced so I'm sorry if this is coming across as blasé! 

Generally the reason I'm not Christian is that I don't believe god can be instantiated in man, additionally, though this might be controversial to you, I also think to grow early Christianity adopted too many pagan (ie Roman) holidays as part of its integration with their tradition (ie some of the festivals are literally reflections or Christianised versions of a Roman pantheon festivals...). I think this helped the religion establish itself in a febrile atmosphere but it's a compromise to the earlier teachings that I'm not sure I agree with (again personally for me). I also believe that Christianity has a lot of variations that split it (fork the religion) too much. I'm not saying Judaism doesn't have this problem (hello extreme versions of Reform Judaism) but generally the baseline is very very consistent

I guess the first century argument I am referencing is just the one outlined in the gospels on how Jesus was arrested and executed on the grounds of blasphemy. As you described, the Jewish tradition would hold that the ultimate Messiah (as opposed to a proximate messiah) is still to come, and that he would be a Davidic king who could not suffer and be debased, much less die. God dying on the cross would on its face be an impossibility with the Jewish understanding of God primarily as essence rather than as hypostasis (though I might be wrong on this point); the divine essence cannot die, and even Christians affirm this, but God truly died on the cross because the person of Jesus, who is God, died on the cross. 

(By the way, Jesus and his followers say that he has a direct bloodline to king David via adoption by Joseph. If we reject adoption as bloodline continuity, many other biblical bloodlines do not work. Moreover, it is said that Jesus taught the Torah himself at age twelve, and so presumably had some ability to master and learn it himself.)

I think the nuance in the Christian view too is that Jesus unites Israel (as opposed to the "Jewish people", which only represents two tribes) through uniting all of mankind and grafting them into Israel. One of the hard questions for rationalists is how to break people out of undesirable or evil equilibria that take the form of everyone needing to stay in a suboptimal Nash equilibrium due to everyone being punished by trying to leave it. The answer is that there is always externality (God, God’s will) except at the very ultimate, and if the ultimate is tautologically good, then the ultimate is the criterion by which all proximate levels are tested. The Emperor’s New Clothes shows the way in which suboptimal equilibria are broken: an outside force comes in to break it. The problem with most rationalist thought experiments is reasoning from a place of lack of externality.

You might be interested in how Christian creativity was expressed through historical methods of musical improvisation. I am not aware of any similarly florid tradition existing in literally any other culture. Actually insane how fruitful it was. The reason why all the common practice era composers were so well-developed in their musical language is because they were doing the equivalent of learning classical Latin natively, but in their fingers and voices.

Partimento is, in my mind, a distinctly Christian craft. It starts from exactly the opposite end of modern music theory which reifies functions and inversions and other abstract categories that are not immediately about “What notes will I play now?”. It makes a lot more sense why Western art music is so powerful when you realized that for these people, they were pretty much able to improvise any specific section of their music. They were practically musical essayist-rhetoricians. Meanwhile, today’s composers are quite similar to non-native English speakers writing the next American novel using Google Translate.

A religion’s extinction is weak disconfirming evidence. A religion’s survival is not confirming evidence, but merely non-disconfirming. The real confirmation would require resurrection at the eschaton, not institutional persistence.

God's law factors in reversals, which is why we can evaluate fruit by what identifies in existence regardless of reversals. A disposition that is resilient to inversion and continuous thwarting is one that is willing to continually walk with Christ. The thing that survives inversion is the thing disposed toward the Logos.

What dies with the Son is raised with him. It is dying in Christ that lets one participate in his resurrection. What identifies with Christ is not only what keeps existing in its current recognizable form, but what keeps obeying in the very act of “losing”.

Teleology is the hidden constant of all experiments. It is also the perfect answer which we receive in the one experiment called reality which is only conclusive at the end. Til then, there is only faith. But when we are there, the higher virtue than faith (and hope) is love.

Empiricism without teleology collapses into voyeurism: a passive staring at phenomena with no criterion for significance. It pretends to honor “facts” while denying the very structure that makes facts intelligible. Without an ordered end, observation becomes accumulation—an archive without argument. Causality becomes habit, law becomes statistical superstition, and explanation decays into correlation. A world without telos is a world that cannot be *known*, only monitored.

Reverse the vector and coherence returns. Teleology anchors intelligibility because ends disclose the form of processes. A thing is grasped when its direction is grasped; its being is luminous in light of its fulfillment. From this, disciplined observation becomes possible, for empiricism is simply the craft of attending to the contours of directed being. To see truly is to see movement toward completion.

Thus teleology makes empiricism not only possible but fruitful. Facts become articulations of form-in-motion; regularities become signatures of purposive order; anomalies become disclosures of deeper structure. Empiricism redeemed by telos becomes apprenticeship in reality’s trajectory. And at the apex of that trajectory stands the Logos, the final cause who renders the world both intelligible and investigable.

From ChatGPT. I was processing a few different notes on how I simultaneously think that it would be mistaken to collapse teleology into empiricism, but also mistaken to not think that empiricism gives us a clearer picture of what is the structure of the teleological kingdom.

Collapsing teleology into empiricism is a category error: the end—the telos—is what gives meaning to the data, not the other way around. Observing phenomena, recording correlations, measuring frequencies—none of that yields the final “why” of existence; it only gives the appearance of order if you treat it as self-sufficient. Without teleology, empiricism is a museum of fragments.

Yet to ignore empiricism entirely is to blind yourself to the scaffolding of the kingdom as it manifests in creation. God ordains structure, and that structure is not random: it bears traces, gradients, and patterns that can be read. Empirical study illuminates these patterns, giving us a map of the avenues by which God’s providence operates, the channels through which his will intersects creation. It does not replace teleology; it clarifies it.

In other words: empiricism is descriptive, teleology is normative. Empiricism sketches the contours of reality; teleology interprets those contours in the light of Christ. The one without the other is incomplete, but together they allow a human intellect to see both the structure and the purpose of creation.

The most serious question, beyond any empirical concerns, is whether we will have our locus of identity in what comes after everything that we can possibly know. This is a question that is in theory empirical, but is actually beyond our epistemic and empirical horizons. And so there is only faith left.

In this regard, empirical observation can help to test our current identities and perhaps edify us, showing us the cases where it seems that we are grasping onto non-existence and thus orienting away from the Lord. We cannot veer to the left or the right. We must by the end of it go exactly into his loving arms. He calls us, and we follow, and we wobble til we are face to face with him.

message:
Love collapses the moment its center of gravity shifts from *reality* to *performance*. The moment the shared “we” rests on anything other than the one God who *is* truth, you get centrifugal force—personalities, projections, politics—each demanding allegiance to a fragment. And fragments cannot sustain communion.

The consensus required for love is not ideological harmony but ontological submission. Not “we agree on propositions,” but “we agree on the Real.” Politics poisons this not because politics is uniquely vile, but because it tempts us toward secondary worlds—constructed, reactive, self-curated. Each faction offers a counterfeit peace: *cohere with us, and we will relieve you of the burden of truth.* It’s erotic precisely in its misdirected teleology: a yearning for unity traded for the intoxication of belonging. Like adultery, it promises intensity at the cost of identity.

Happy families being “one way” is simply the domestic corollary of divine simplicity: the good is simple, the false is endlessly recombinant. One reality yields one mode of communion. Many illusions yield many ways of fracturing.

And you’re right—communion is discerned not by shared shibboleths but by shared dependence. The sacrament with your wife is not a symbol of unity; it is unity, because it is participation in the One. To gesture at signs instead of the substance is to treat love as semiotics rather than ontology. Anyone can want you to say the right thing. Fewer can want you to mean the right thing. Rarest of all are those who want you to *lean on* the right thing—the God who guarantees that consensus is not negotiable but given.

Love requires coincidence of life in the same God. Everything else is the politics of exile.
